claim: For an AI security talk specifically: map threats onto risk categories the audience already owns (data protection, third-party, compliance, reputation) and translate findings into dollars + a decision the audience must make

r an AI security talk specifically: map threats onto risk categories the audience already owns (data protection, third-party, compliance, reputation) and translate findings into dollars + a decision the audience must make
| Lead with BLUF | First 2–3 sentences carry situation, complication, and your recommendation
| Lead with BLUF | First 2–3 sentences carry situation, complication, and your recommendation ) | Executives are problem-solvers; they decide whether to keep listening in ~30 seconds
| Audience is the hero, you're the mentor | Use Duarte's Sparkline: toggle between "what is" and "what could be," ending on a call to action
| One analogy per concept, no jargon | "Prompt injection is SQL injection for English" — anchor every term in something they already know

| One analogy per concept, no jargon | "Prompt injection is SQL injection for English" — anchor every term in something they already know | Buzzwords and acronyms make non-technical audiences tune out fast
| 10-7 rule | Identify the most essential 10% of the message, repeat it ~7 times across the talk
| One idea per slide, visual over text | ≤3–4 short bullets, replace bullets with a diagram when systems are involved
| One idea per slide, visual over text | ≤3–4 short bullets, replace bullets with a diagram when systems are involved
- Translation into money. "We found 47 unauthorized AI tools" lands as noise; "$8.4M in exposure via 47 AI tools touching customer PII" lands as a decision
- Mapping to risk categories they already own. Data protection, third-party/vendor risk, compliance, and reputation — AI rarely needs a new bucket
- Mapping to risk categories they already own. Data protection, third-party/vendor risk, compliance, and reputation — AI rarely needs a new bucket
- Practical risks before exotic ones. Employees pasting customer data into public LLMs beats data-poisoning and model theft as your opener — it's what's happening this week
- Take a position. Boards want to know what you recommend, not a balanced survey of options
- Take a position. Boards want to know what you recommend, not a balanced survey of options
Watch faces in the first three minutes — confused → slow down; nodding → advance
